The San Francisco-based online retailer specializes in selling These two things, he says, have enabled the retailer to enter its own brand of low-cost “luxury” goods, the kinds of things product categories far more quickly than a traditional model designed to lessen the tension between your aspirations and would allow. Now, Quince is going even further—beyond its your budget. The company is building its brand on a lineup of white-label assortment, selling third-party products from more traditional high-end brands, including $260 bottles of Dom Pérignon Champagne and $315 Augustinus Bader moisturizer. These aren’t good deals; some of the stuff, like the moisturizer, is sold at the manufacturer’s suggested retail price. But they’re useful for Quince, both as inventory that can draw in new affluent customers and as recog- nizable status symbols that can boost the brand’s overall luxury aura. To do all this, Quince has pulled off at least a couple of things that would have seemed improbable for any company even a few years ago.
